items.q: Why do text types like letters and articles have predictable features?
items.correct: Because their conventions, such as a headline or sign-off, help readers recognise the form and purpose.
items.misconception: Because writers must rigidly copy a template for no real reason.
items.random_wrong: Because every text type uses identical features.
items.why: Students treat form conventions as arbitrary rather than aids to communication.

items.q: How should the form of a transactional text be chosen?
items.correct: To match the purpose and audience, e.g. a formal letter to complain or a leaflet to inform.
items.misconception: Any form works equally well for any task.
items.random_wrong: The form is chosen only by counting the words required.
items.why: Learners overlook that form must suit purpose and audience.

items.q: What is the role of a counter-argument in persuasive writing?
items.correct: To acknowledge an opposing view and then refute it, strengthening your own case.
items.misconception: A counter-argument weakens your writing and should be avoided.
items.random_wrong: A counter-argument means writing the whole piece twice.
items.why: Students fear that raising opposing views undermines their argument, when handling them strengthens it.

items.q: What does a letter to unknown recipient begin with?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dear Sir/Madam. “Hi” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Topic. “Closing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many key features (FPA) of transactional writing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 features. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
